runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 4 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates
Congressional Budget Office (CBO) microsimulation substitution elasticity assumptions
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD-style tax-benefit simulation elasticity calibrations
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally have low labor supply elasticities. For decile 4 (lower-middle part of the earnings distribution), substitution elasticities in tax-benefit microsimulations are typically calibrated in the range of 0.1–0.25. CBO and similar models often use values around 0.1–0.2 for primary earners on the intensive margin, with some variation by income level. Decile 4 workers may have slightly higher elasticities than top earners due to more binding constraints and proximity to phase-in/phase-out ranges of tax credits (e.g., EITC), but primary earners as a group are relatively inelastic. I center my estimate at 0.15 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and empirical estimates.

(2007) - EUROMOD-style tax-benefit simulation elasticity calibrations"], "reasoning_summary": "Primary earners generally have low labor supply elasticities. For decile 4 (lower-middle part of the earnings distribution), substitution elasticities in tax-benefit microsimulations are typically calibrated in the range of 0.1\u20130.25. Decile 4 workers may have slightly higher elasticities than top earners due to more binding constraints and proximity to phase-in/phase-out ranges of tax credits (e.g., EITC), but primary earners as a group are relatively inelastic. I center my estimate at 0.15 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and empirical estimates."}

citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of Taxable Income
CBO microsimulation labor supply parameters

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally have low labor supply elasticities. For decile 4 (lower-middle earnings), substitution elasticities in tax-benefit microsimulations are typically modest but slightly higher than for top earners. The canonical literature (Gruber-Saez, Chetty et al.) places compensated/substitution elasticities for primary earners in the range of 0.1–0.3, with lower-middle deciles somewhat below average. CBO and similar microsimulation models often use values around 0.1–0.25 for this group. I center my estimate at 0.15, reflecting that decile 4 workers are mostly full-time with limited intensive-margin flexibility, but with some response capacity given proximity to phase-in/phase-out ranges of tax credits (e.g., EITC). Uncertainty spans from 0.05 to 0.35 to account for model specification differences.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 4 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates
CBO microsimulation assumptions for labor supply elasticities
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD-style tax-benefit microsimulation elasticity calibrations
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally have low substitution elasticities (typically 0.1–0.3 in the literature). Decile 4 represents lower-middle earnings, where workers are often in relatively inelastic occupations but may face high effective marginal tax rates due to phase-outs of transfer programs. In U.S. microsimulation models (e.g., CBO, TRIM, or academic TAXSIM-linked models), the substitution elasticity for primary earners in this range is typically calibrated around 0.1–0.25, somewhat higher than for top earners but modest. Anchoring on Gruber-Saez style estimates and CBO's conventional assumptions, I center my belief at 0.15 with moderate uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.35.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally have low substitution elasticities (0.1–0.3 range in the literature). Decile 4 represents lower-middle earnings, where workers are mostly full-time but may have some hours flexibility. runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally have low substitution elasticities (0.1–0.3 range in the literature). Decile 4 workers are in the lower-middle part of the earnings distribution, where labor supply responses tend to be modest but potentially slightly higher than top earners due to proximity to phase-in/phase-out regions of tax credits (e.g., EITC). U.S. microsimulation models like those used by CBO or TRIM typically calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities in the 0.1–0.25 range. Gruber and Saez find compensated elasticities around 0.12–0.40 depending on specification; for primary earners at decile 4, a value near 0.15 is a reasonable central estimate. The bounded relative-change formulation used in some microsimulations can compress or slightly alter the effective parameter, but the order of magnitude remains similar.
